story:
Judge Harold Bribar, a celebrity in robes with a gavel as famous as his eyebrows, presides over Mars’s “Case of the Millennium.” The defendant, Marissa Tautle—legendary burglar, ex-con, and now her own showy attorney—has turned the trial into appointment viewing. The prosecutor, Veronica Brief, is the calm counterpoint: disciplined, meticulous, forgettable in headlines but formidable in court.

The crime seems open-and-shut. Someone stole the Twin Pins of Mars—diamond-encrusted spikes that capped the first city’s oxygen dome—during a gala concert. A foam bomb used as a distraction injured three bystanders, adding assault charges. Tautle attended the event. She bought the needed chemicals. One of the two pins was found under her bed. The second remains missing. Veronica’s case is a fortress of witnesses, texts, video, and physical evidence; the fear isn’t proof but the jury’s appetite for spectacle.

On the final day, Tautle rises in maroon and confidence. She opens by acknowledging the worst fact against her—the pin in her apartment—then bathes it in jokes, grievance, and disbelief: Would a “master criminal” stash the key evidence in the most obvious place? She projects witness quotes that repeatedly call her a “witch,” nudging the jury to notice a pattern of contempt and suggesting, without saying so outright, that testimonies have been coached or colored by bias. She leans into the missing second pin: if she took both, why hide one stupidly and the other impossibly? She stops short of naming an alternative culprit but invites suspicion that evidence was planted.

Veronica, who decided weeks ago to conserve objections and let Tautle overreach, watches the jury: attentive, mixed expressions, not swooning. She remembers failed plea talks—Tautle once dangled the second pin’s location for protection—and bristles at any hint the prosecution is corrupt. Still, even she has to admit Tautle is compelling: a quick study, shamelessly theatrical, always riding the line between charm and contempt.

Then Tautle pushes closer—literally. She prowls the well, approaches Veronica, then the bench, red jacket flaring like a warning. Judge Bribar demands distance. Tautle retreats, spins, and with a crier’s flourish declares the “true mastermind” is in the courtroom. The air knots. A scream. Rustling. Eyes everywhere. And Veronica’s photographic memory snaps: the judge’s famous gavel is missing; it vanished right after Tautle set her hands on the bench.

Veronica moves. So does a stone-faced bailiff. They reach Tautle as the room devolves toward riot, and Veronica fishes the gavel from the defendant’s sleeve. She flips it back to the bench with theatrical restraint. Bribar hammers order out of the chaos and orders Tautle taken into custody. In the sudden hush, he whispers to Veronica in chambers-speak: this may be a mistrial.

Why pull a stunt that risks contempt instead of freedom? Not for legal advantage—the evidence remains heavy, security will tighten, and the assault count is unforgiving—but for oxygen. Tautle thrives on the stage. She fired her lawyers, turned errors into learning beats, and made seven weeks of proceedings her longest, loudest heist yet: stealing time, attention, and the spotlight itself. Even if a new trial awaits, she’s already taken what she came for.
